request_summary: Hero Academy MVP: a superhero-themed 11+ learning platform for 8-10 year olds. Three portals (child, parent, admin), assessment engine, gamification, deferred payments. React + TypeScript + Vite SPA frontend, Node/Express REST API, SQLite, JWT auth.

items.title: Auth & Identity
items.outcome: All three portals can authenticate their respective user roles securely; JWTs are issued and validated consistently; sessions expire and refresh correctly.
scope: Parent registration, email verification, login, password reset
Child PIN login with avatar selection; short-lived in-memory JWT
Admin login (email/password)
JWT issuance: access token (15 min) + refresh token (HttpOnly cookie, 7 days, rotated on use)
Role claims: parent | child | admin
Route-level middleware enforcing role guards
Refresh token revocation list in DB for logout/suspend flows
PIN rate-limiting (5 attempts per child per 10 min)
non_goals: Admin account creation flow (see BC-06 or discovery)
assumptions: Admin accounts are seeded via seed script; no invite flow in MVP (OQ-8 unresolved — discovery issue created as BC-06-DQ)
Email verification for parents is non-blocking vs blocking is unresolved (OQ-3) — discovery issue created
Child JWT carries parent_id claim to enable subscription gating without extra DB lookup
items.split_rationale: Auth is a cross-portal service but is owned by a single backend module; keeping it as one bounded context avoids fragmentation across the three portals.

reasons: Three auth flows, but all share the same JWT infrastructure and middleware pattern

items.title: Child Portal — Learning & Gameplay
items.outcome: A child can log in with their PIN, navigate the city map, select a district, run a mission, and see their hero profile with updated XP and rank.
scope: PIN + avatar selection login flow (child-facing)
City map UI: zone/district unlock state, navigation
District view and mission selection
Mission runner: question render, answer capture, attempt submission, feedback animation
Hero profile: avatar, XP bar, rank, stats, achievements display
2-hour inactivity auto-logout (client-side idle timer)
In-memory JWT storage (no localStorage)
Mobile-first responsive layout (375px+), touch targets ≥ 44px
Framer Motion animations: city fly-in, district zoom, power-up flash
non_goals: Assessment scoring logic (owned by Assessment Engine BC-04)
assumptions: Single Vite SPA code-split by portal (AD-7)
Avatar config JSON schema must be agreed before component build (OQ-5 — discovery issue created)
City map unlock state is read from API; no offline caching in MVP
items.split_rationale: Child portal is a distinct UX surface with its own auth model, animation layer, and gameplay interactions; separating it from parent and admin portals keeps ownership clean.

reasons: Spans city map, mission runner, hero profile, and auth flow — each is a meaningful delivery slice
Will require multiple PRs and likely multiple owners

items.title: Parent Portal
items.outcome: A parent can register, verify their email, manage their children's profiles, and view each child's progress dashboard.
scope: Parent registration and email verification flow
Parent login, logout, password reset
Child management: add, view, edit, remove child profiles
Child progress dashboard: missions completed, scores, XP, rank, streak, strongest/weakest district
Subscription status display (plan badge — free for MVP)
Mobile-readable functional dashboard UI
non_goals: Parent approval of child activity (OQ-9 — out of MVP scope)
assumptions: Parent portal is read-only for child progress data; no activity approval workflow in MVP (OQ-9)
Email verification blocking behaviour to be resolved via discovery (OQ-3)
Subscription UI shows plan status read from local DB; no Stripe calls in MVP
items.split_rationale: Parent portal is a distinct bounded context owning account management and child management; it consumes progress data but does not own it.

reasons: Functional dashboard UI; no heavy animation; auth flows shared with BC-01

items.title: Assessment Engine
items.outcome: The system can serve ordered question sets to a child, accept and score attempt submissions, compute stars and XP earned, and enforce subscription gating.
scope: Question bank: CRUD (admin-facing), read (child-facing)
Assessment definitions: CRUD (admin-facing), fetch by topic (child-facing)
Attempt submission: POST attempt → score answers → compute stars (1/2/3) and XP earned → persist attempt
Attempt deduplication via client-generated UUID (unique constraint)
Subscription gating: read plan field from subscriptions table; stub to free for MVP
Question ordering: fixed vs randomised per attempt (OQ-6 — must be resolved before build)
non_goals: Admin UI for question/assessment management (owned by Admin Portal BC-06)
Stripe or billing (owned by Payments BC-07)
assumptions: Question ordering (random vs fixed) must be decided before implementation — OQ-6 is a blocker
Subscription gating logic is implemented against plan field even in MVP to reduce post-MVP migration cost (OQ-7 recommendation accepted)
15 questions per assessment at 11+ standard; seed data provides 150 questions across 5 topics
items.split_rationale: Assessment Engine owns the question bank, attempt lifecycle, and scoring. Separating it from gamification keeps the scoring contract clean and independently testable.

reasons: Backend-only bounded context; CRUD + scoring logic is well-scoped

items.title: Gamification
items.outcome: After each completed attempt, XP is accumulated, rank tier transitions are evaluated, streaks are tracked, and achievements are awarded; child stats are exposed to both the child and parent portals.
scope: XP delta computation and persistence after attempt submission
Rank tier evaluation and transition on XP milestone
Streak tracking (consecutive daily missions)
Achievement badge evaluation and award
Child stats API: total missions, average score, streak, strongest/weakest district
In-process service triggered as post-attempt side-effect within attempt submission handler
non_goals: Gamification UI (owned by Child Portal BC-02 and Parent Portal BC-03)
Attempt scoring (owned by Assessment Engine BC-04)
Leaderboards or social features (not in MVP)
assumptions: Gamification runs as an in-process service, not a separate module or process (AD-5)
Rank tier definitions (XP thresholds) must be specified before implementation
Achievement definitions (triggers and badge metadata) must be specified before implementation
items.split_rationale: Gamification is a distinct domain with its own rules (XP, rank, streak, achievements) that is consumed by multiple portals. Keeping it separate from the Assessment Engine preserves a clean scoring/gamification boundary.

reasons: Backend service layer only; no UI ownership; well-bounded side-effect chain

items.title: Admin Portal
items.outcome: An admin can manage questions and assessments, view and soft-delete user accounts, and access the system with appropriate access controls.
scope: Admin login (email/password)
Question management: create, read, update, soft-delete
Assessment management: create, read, update, soft-delete; assign questions
User management: list parents and children, soft-delete accounts
Payment overview display (read-only plan status; no Stripe in MVP)
Desktop-oriented data-dense table/form UI (768px minimum)
Admin role guard middleware on all admin routes
non_goals: Content delivery to children (owned by Assessment Engine BC-04)
assumptions: Admin accounts are created via seed script only for MVP — no invite or self-registration flow (OQ-8 unresolved; discovery item raised)
Soft-delete is the deletion strategy for all user and content records
Admin portal is desktop-oriented; mobile-first not required
items.split_rationale: Admin portal is a distinct bounded context with its own UX pattern and access model; separating it from the Assessment Engine avoids coupling content management to content delivery.

reasons: CRUD-heavy but well-patterned; desktop-only UI reduces responsive complexity

items.title: Payments (Deferred Seam)
items.outcome: The architecture seam for Stripe integration exists and is stubbed; the subscriptions table and gating logic are in place so billing can be activated post-MVP without a structural retrofit.
scope: Subscriptions table seeded with free plan for all parents
Stripe webhook endpoint stubbed: POST /api/v1/webhooks/stripe returns 200 and logs event
Plan field read by Assessment Engine gating logic (stubbed to free)
No Stripe SDK calls in MVP
assumptions: Stripe is the chosen payment provider post-MVP
All MVP users are on the free plan; no gating enforcement required beyond reading the plan field
Webhook signature verification is not implemented in MVP stub
items.split_rationale: Payments is architecturally isolated by design. Treating it as a stub bounded context now prevents it from bleeding into the Assessment Engine or Parent Portal.

reasons: MVP scope is stub-only; full Stripe integration is post-MVP

items.title: Infrastructure, Deployment & Data Persistence
items.outcome: The application can be deployed to a single server, the database schema is versioned and migrations run on startup, seed data is idempotent, and nightly backups protect the SQLite file.
scope: Project structure: monorepo layout for /client and /server
Express serves /client/dist as static assets; SPA fallback for non-/api/* routes
Vite dev server proxying /api/* to Express in development
SQLite schema migration runner (versioned migration files applied at startup)
WAL mode enabled on SQLite
Idempotent DB seed script: 1 subject, 5 topics, 15 assessments, 150 questions at 11+ standard
Process supervision (PM2 or systemd)
Nightly SQLite backup to object storage
Environment configuration (dotenv or equivalent)
non_goals: CDN, load balancer, or multi-server deployment
CI/CD pipeline beyond basic build verification
assumptions: Deployment target is unresolved (OQ-2 — discovery issue raised); VPS/Render/Railway are plausible targets
Single Node.js process for API + static serving (AD-1)
SQLite with better-sqlite3 (AD-2); DB file on persistent volume
items.split_rationale: Infrastructure concerns cross all bounded contexts and must be established before any portal or engine can be built end-to-end.

reasons: Well-understood setup tasks; no novel infrastructure work required for MVP

items.title: Data Privacy & Compliance
items.outcome: Child PII is minimised and protected; parent PII is not exposed in child API responses; GDPR/UK data protection obligations are tracked and a privacy policy is in place before public launch.
scope: Child records store hero name + PIN hash only — no email, no surname
Parent email must not appear in child-facing API responses
bcrypt hashing for parent/admin passwords and child PINs
GDPR note: data retention policy and privacy policy page required before public launch (not MVP launch blocker, but must be tracked)
Data controller identification (OQ-10)
non_goals: Cookie consent banner implementation (tracked but deferred)
Right-to-erasure automated workflow (manual acceptable for MVP)
assumptions: Deployment jurisdiction is UK/EU; GDPR applies
Privacy policy is a pre-launch legal requirement but not a blocker for MVP build
Data controller is the product owner — must be confirmed (OQ-10)
items.split_rationale: Privacy obligations cross all bounded contexts (auth, child portal, parent portal, assessment data). Owning it as a cross-cutting concern ensures it is not dropped between domain epics.

reasons: MVP scope is constraint enforcement and tracking; full compliance work is post-launch

items.title: Resolve blocking open questions before detailed design
items.outcome: All blocking open questions are answered so that child portal, assessment engine, auth, and admin portal detailed design can proceed without guessing.
scope: OQ-1: Single Vite SPA vs three separate builds (AD-7 decision made — confirm and close)
OQ-2: Deployment target (VPS / Railway / Render / Fly.io) — affects infra setup
OQ-3: Email verification blocking vs non-blocking for MVP — affects auth flow
OQ-5: Avatar config JSON schema — must be agreed before frontend avatar components and DB layer are built
OQ-6: Question ordering per attempt (fixed vs randomised) — affects attempt scoring logic
OQ-8: Admin account creation — seed script only or invite flow?
non_goals: Non-blocking questions (OQ-4, OQ-7, OQ-9, OQ-10) — tracked but not blockers
assumptions: OQ-1 is likely closed by AD-7 (single SPA) but should be formally confirmed
OQ-5 (avatar config schema) is a hard blocker for BC-02 detailed design
items.split_rationale: Grouping unresolved blocking questions into a discovery parent prevents downstream epics from being built on guesses. Each question may spawn a short spike or product decision.

reasons: Discovery work; not implementation; should be resolved quickly before detailed design proceeds
